global.title: Collaboration uses a mesh of per-user owner-only feeds because only the feed owner can write to their own Swarm feed
global.content:
## Why a mesh of per-user feeds is the only option

Swarm enforces a hard constraint at the protocol level: **only the owner can write to their own feed**. There is no shared multi-writer feed primitive. This rules out the naive "one collaborative feed everyone appends to" data model.

Collaboration therefore must be structured as a **mesh of per-user feeds that everyone reads**. Each collaborator owns and writes their own set of topics; every other collaborator subscribes to and pulls from those peer-owned feeds.

Concretely, each collaborator gets their own ACT-protected feeds:

```
Topic: ph:v2:collab:<collabId>:ops:<driveId>         (drive-level ops)
       ph:v2:collab:<collabId>:ops:<driveId>:<docId> (doc-level ops)
Owner: each collaborator owns their own topic(s)
ACT:   grantees = all participants' Bee node pubkeys
```

Each collaborator appends their own ops to their own feeds (debounced + batched, same pipeline as the existing SwarmChannel push). On the read side, every participant registers pull sources for every *other* participant's collab feeds. This mesh topology is a direct consequence of the owner-only-write rule and shapes the rest of the design (GSOC pings per peer, cursor-per-peer-source, etc.).
global.description: Swarm's feed rule (only owner can write to own feed) forces collab to be a mesh of per-user feeds that everyone reads, not a single shared feed.
